cards.title: AI's Code Spree: We're Drowning in Output, Not Shipping Faster
cards.thesis: AI is making it super easy to write code and generate app features, but it's creating massive bottlenecks when it comes to actually reviewing, debugging, and deploying that code. Developers are feeling faster at typing, but their projects aren't hitting the finish line any quicker because the human side of the loop (like code reviews) and the agent side (like ensuring AI agents don't crash) can't keep up.

evidence.text: Organizations are facing a 'flooding and surge in PRs' (Pull Requests – proposed code changes) because AI multiplies code output. Developers feel 'velocity gains in programming' but it's 'not resulting into eventual productivity or velocity in roadmap.'
People are asking about the 'state of app development in 2026' and how AI is 'affecting app development,' showing a broad awareness and concern about AI's impact on how apps are built.

Builders are struggling with 'worst war stories bringing agentic applications into prod' (getting AI agents, which are programs that act semi-autonomously, to work reliably in real-world systems). They report 'cascading errors' and 'almost no visibility' when an AI agent fails mid-way.

Some developers admit they 'don’t have much knowledge about the best way to architect applications' and often just 'go with whatever the agent recommends,' highlighting a potential quality and design gap in AI-generated code.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's focused on making AI *write more code* and *build more agents*, but the real bottleneck is what happens *after* the AI generates it. Builders are drowning in AI-generated pull requests and their AI agents are failing in production with 'almost no visibility' into why. The killer move right now is building a 'copilot for the copilot' — a tool that helps humans understand, manage, and debug the explosion of AI-created output. Think a simple dashboard that visualizes agent workflows, flags common failure points, or intelligently summarizes AI-generated code changes into a human-digestible format, so teams can actually ship AI-driven features reliably.

cards.title: AI's Next Level: Making Anything Editable
cards.thesis: AI is no longer just generating images or text; it's automating the *process* of creating detailed, structured content. These new 'AI skills' (like an AI following a multi-step recipe to complete a task) can turn raw ideas or visual references into highly organized, editable outputs, from detailed creative prompts to fully editable presentations.

evidence.text: A GitHub project with 140 engagements uses an AI 'skill' to direct and expand detailed prompts for generating AI female portraits, showing how AI can automate complex creative instructions.

Another project with 57 engagements uses an AI 'skill' to rebuild editable PowerPoint presentations from simple screenshots or reference images. It generates visual references, keeps complex parts like illustrations, and reconstructs editable elements like text boxes and shapes for titles and body content.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's trying to make AI generate *new* stuff from scratch, but these projects show the real power is making *existing* visual content editable and structured. Nobody's built a dead-simple tool that takes a screenshot of *any* app or website and turns it into editable Figma components or clean HTML/CSS. This is prime for a browser extension that lets designers or developers quickly grab UI ideas and make them their own, saving hours of manual rebuilding.
